content.text: What Are Sovereign and Government Clouds?

Sovereign clouds are physically and logically isolated versions of the Azure platform, built to meet the strictest government and national security data sovereignty requirements. They are not simply a different configuration of commercial Azure — they run on separate hardware, separate network infrastructure, and are operated by a separate staff with specific clearance or legal requirements.

While commercial Azure serves the broadest set of customers and operates the most regions, sovereign clouds exist for cases where data must never mix with commercial customer workloads and where the operator of the infrastructure must satisfy national-level trust requirements. As of 2024, Azure operates two primary sovereign cloud environments: Azure Government (for US government and approved contractors) and Azure China (operated by 21Vianet for the Chinese market).

A sovereign cloud is not a 'more secure' version of commercial Azure — it is a legally separate instance, operated under different contracts, with different staff, different regulatory certifications, and different API endpoints. You cannot access it with a commercial Azure account.

attrs.alt: Azure Sovereign Cloud Isolation: commercial Azure on the left, physically separated from Azure Government (centre) and Azure China (right), each with distinct regions, compliance certifications, and access requirements

content.text: The isolation between commercial Azure and sovereign clouds is not software-defined — it is physical. Separate racks, separate network switches, separate power feeds, separate software pipelines. The diagram above illustrates this at a high level: even though they share the Azure brand and much of the same underlying technology, they are completely separate clouds.

Azure Government is available to US federal, state, local, and tribal government entities, as well as contractors, partners, and vendors who process data on their behalf. Eligibility is validated during account provisioning — you cannot accidentally sign up for Azure Government on a personal Microsoft account.
Azure Government datacentres are located in the continental United States. Physical access is restricted to US persons (citizens and lawful permanent residents).

Support, operations, and engineering staff who can access Azure Government infrastructure must be US persons and undergo additional background screening. Microsoft employees who are not US citizens cannot access Azure Government systems.
Government customers have their own AAD tenants that are isolated from commercial AAD. A Microsoft account registered in commercial AAD cannot access Azure Government resources.
The portal is at portal.azure.us (not portal.azure.com). The ARM endpoint is management.usgovcloudapi.net. The CLI requires az cloud set --name AzureUSGovernment before login.
Highest Federal Risk and Authorization Management Program level. Required for sensitive but unclassified (SBU) federal data.
Department of Defense Cloud Computing Security Requirements Guide Level 2 — public-facing DoD info
Controlled Unclassified Information (CUI) for DoD — non-national security
Required for contractor systems with CUI
Higher-sensitivity CUI including National Security Systems supporting non-mission-critical functions
Criminal Justice Information Services — required for law enforcement data (FBI fingerprints, criminal records)
International Traffic in Arms Regulations — export-controlled defence articles and technical data
Tax return data handling requirements for agencies handling federal tax information

USDoD Central and USDoD East have even stricter isolation than the USGov regions — they are designated specifically for Department of Defense workloads at IL5. Not all Azure services available in USGov regions are available in USDoD regions.
Connecting to Azure Government via CLI

# Switch Azure CLI to Azure Government cloud
az cloud set --name AzureUSGovernment

# Login with government credentials
az login --tenant <government-tenant-id>

# Verify you are targeting the government cloud
az cloud show --query "{Name:name, ResourceManager:endpoints.resourceManager}" --output json
# Expected: "management.usgovcloudapi.net"

# List available government regions
az account list-locations \
  --query "[].{Name:name, DisplayName:displayName}" \
  --output table
# Returns: usgovvirginia, usgovtexas, usgovarizona, usdodcentral, usdodeast

# Deploy a resource group in Azure Government
az group create \
  --name agency-prod-rg \
  --location usgovvirginia

# Return to commercial cloud
az cloud set --name AzureCloud

# Python SDK: connect to Azure Government
from azure.identity import ClientSecretCredential
from azure.mgmt.resource import ResourceManagementClient
from azure.mgmt.resource.resources import ResourceManagementClient
import azure.identity as ai

# Azure Government uses a different authority URL
AUTHORITY = "https://login.microsoftonline.us/<gov-tenant-id>"
RESOURCE = "https://management.usgovcloudapi.net/"

credential = ClientSecretCredential(
    tenant_id="<gov-tenant-id>",
    client_id="<app-registration-client-id>",
    client_secret="<client-secret>",
    authority=AUTHORITY  # Government authority, not commercial
)

# ARM endpoint for Azure Government
client = ResourceManagementClient(
    credential=credential,
    subscription_id="<gov-subscription-id>",
    base_url="https://management.usgovcloudapi.net/"
)

# List resource groups in the government subscription
for rg in client.resource_groups.list():
    print(f"{rg.name}: {rg.location}")
Legal Structure and Why It's Different

Azure China is not operated by Microsoft. Under Chinese telecommunications law, foreign companies cannot directly operate internet services or cloud infrastructure in China. Microsoft's solution was to licence the Azure technology stack to 21Vianet, a Chinese company, which operates the datacentres, employs the staff, and handles the customer relationship under a separate legal entity.

This has practical consequences: Microsoft can take technical measures to ensure its software runs on 21Vianet's infrastructure, but 21Vianet staff — not Microsoft employees — have physical and operational access to the China datacentres. Data in Azure China is subject to Chinese law, including the Cybersecurity Law, Data Security Law, and Personal Information Protection Law (PIPL).
You must have a registered Chinese business entity. Individuals and foreign companies without a China subsidiary cannot directly sign up — they must partner with a Chinese legal entity.

Any website or online service hosted in China must complete an Internet Content Provider (ICP) registration with MIIT (Ministry of Industry and Information Technology). Running a production workload without ICP filing is illegal in China.
Account management, billing, and support go through 21Vianet, not Microsoft. The portal is at portal.azure.cn; billing is in RMB (CNY); support tickets go to 21Vianet support teams.

PIPL requires that personal data of Chinese citizens not be transferred outside China without explicit consent and a Security Assessment by CAC (Cyberspace Administration of China). Azure China keeps data in China by design.
Azure China offers approximately 70–80% of the services available in commercial Azure. Key differences:
Azure OpenAI Service: available in Azure China but with model restrictions and separate content policies; GPT-4o and some newer models may have a time lag before availability.
GitHub Actions: not available in Azure China; use Azure Pipelines (Azure DevOps) instead.
Some AI services (Translator, Speech, Vision) are available but may have different model versions than commercial Azure.
Azure CDN in China uses 21Vianet's CDN network rather than Akamai/Verizon partners used in commercial Azure.
Microsoft Defender for Cloud and Sentinel are available with some feature differences.

# Azure CLI: switch to Azure China
az cloud set --name AzureChinaCloud

# Login with 21Vianet account credentials
az login --tenant <china-tenant-id>

# Check the cloud environment
az cloud show --query "{Name:name, ResourceManager:endpoints.resourceManager}" --output json
# Expected: "management.chinacloudapi.cn"

# List Azure China regions
az account list-locations \
  --query "[].{Name:name, DisplayName:displayName}" \
  --output table

# Create a resource group in China North 3 (newest region)
az group create \
  --name china-prod-rg \
  --location chinanorth3

# Deploy a storage account (China-compliant, data stays in China)
az storage account create \
  --name chinaprodstg \
  --resource-group china-prod-rg \
  --location chinanorth3 \
  --sku Standard_ZRS \           # GRS replicates to China East 3 — stays in China
  --kind StorageV2

# Return to commercial Azure
az cloud set --name AzureCloud
Choosing Between Commercial and Sovereign Cloud
The decision between commercial Azure and a sovereign cloud is almost always driven by regulatory mandate, not preference:
US federal agency (SBU data, FedRAMP High required)
FedRAMP High only available in Azure Government
IL4 certification; screened Microsoft staff requirement
Chinese company serving Chinese users (personal data)
PIPL compliance requires data to stay in China
Commercial Azure (EU regions for EU data, US regions for US data)
No sovereignty requirement beyond standard GDPR/CCPA
NHS DSPT and UK GDPR satisfied by commercial Azure UK regions; sovereign cloud not required
DORA compliance satisfied by commercial Azure; no sovereign cloud needed

Many organisations incorrectly assume they need a sovereign cloud for compliance. Most commercial compliance frameworks (GDPR, PCI DSS, SOC 2, HIPAA) are satisfied by commercial Azure with the correct configuration. Sovereign cloud is for government-mandated compliance levels (FedRAMP High, DoD CC SRG IL4/IL5, CJIS, ITAR) or for China-specific data localisation under PIPL.
Beyond the access requirements, sovereign clouds have engineering implications that affect how you architect and deploy solutions:

Resource properties and API versions may differ. An ARM template that works in commercial Azure may require modifications for Azure Government (different endpoint URLs, slightly different API versions for some services).
A commercial Azure VNet cannot peer with an Azure Government VNet. Cross-cloud connectivity requires ExpressRoute circuits to on-premises and back, or managed solutions like Azure Arc.
Azure Arc for hybrid sovereign management:
Azure Arc can project on-premises and non-Azure resources into Azure Government for centralised policy and monitoring, allowing sovereign cloud management of on-premises servers.

New Azure services launch in commercial Azure first; Azure Government typically receives them months later. Plan your architecture around services that are GA in Azure Government if they are required.
Azure Government SLAs are the same as commercial Azure for equivalent service tiers.
Working Across Commercial and Sovereign Clouds with Azure Arc

Many organisations need to manage a mix of commercial Azure, Azure Government, and on-premises infrastructure from a single control plane. Azure Arc extends Azure management capabilities to non-Azure resources — including resources in sovereign clouds — by projecting them as Azure Resource Manager objects.

The most common use case is an organisation that runs most workloads in commercial Azure but has a classified enclave in Azure Government. Azure Arc cannot bridge the two clouds directly (they remain isolated), but it can surface on-premises infrastructure in Azure Government as Arc-enabled servers, allowing uniform policy, monitoring, and GitOps deployment across the entire government footprint.

# Register an on-premises server with Azure Government via Azure Arc
# (Run this script on the on-premises server; it connects to the Azure Government ARM endpoint)

# Step 1: Switch CLI to Azure Government
az cloud set --name AzureUSGovernment
az login --tenant <gov-tenant-id>

# Step 2: Download and run the Arc onboarding script
az connectedmachine install-agent \
  --resource-group agency-arc-rg \
  --location usgovvirginia

# Alternatively, generate a one-time onboarding script for Windows/Linux
az connectedmachine generate-install-script \
  --resource-group agency-arc-rg \
  --location usgovvirginia \
  --subscription <gov-subscription-id> \
  --service-principal-id <sp-client-id> \
  --service-principal-secret <sp-secret> \
  --tenant-id <gov-tenant-id> \
  --output-file ./onboard-arc.ps1   # Run on the on-premises Windows server

# Step 3: After onboarding, apply Azure Policy to the Arc-enabled server
# (same policy format as native Azure VMs)
az policy assignment create \
  --name "arc-server-cis-benchmark" \
  --policy "<cis-benchmark-policy-id>" \
  --scope /subscriptions/<gov-sub>/resourceGroups/agency-arc-rg \
  --assign-identity \
  --location usgovvirginia
FedRAMP Authorization: What It Means for Developers

FedRAMP (Federal Risk and Authorization Management Program) is a US government-wide programme that standardises security assessment, authorisation, and continuous monitoring of cloud services. Understanding FedRAMP impacts how Azure Government workloads are architected and how developers write code for them.
Public data, low impact on disclosure
Controlled Unclassified Information (CUI) with moderate impact
High impact CUI — disclosure could cause severe harm
Law enforcement, health records, emergency services

FedRAMP High only covers Azure services that have completed the FedRAMP High assessment. Not every Azure service available in commercial Azure has been assessed for FedRAMP High in Azure Government. Developers building government workloads must check the FedRAMP Marketplace to confirm that every service in their architecture has the required authorisation.

# Practical FedRAMP-aware development: check service availability in Azure Government
# The Azure products by region page lists service availability, but for FedRAMP status
# check marketplace.fedramp.gov for the official authorisation scope

# Example: verify that Azure Kubernetes Service is available in USGov Virginia
az aks get-versions \
  --location usgovvirginia \
  --output table
# If this returns results, AKS is available in that government region

# Check what Cognitive Services capabilities are available in government
az cognitiveservices account list-kinds \
  --output table
# Note: some AI services available in commercial Azure are not available or
# are available at a different API version in Azure Government

# Configure Terraform to deploy to Azure Government
cat > providers.tf << 'EOF'
terraform {
  required_providers {
    azurerm = {
      source  = "hashicorp/azurerm"
      version = "~> 3.0"
    }
  }
}

provider "azurerm" {
  environment     = "usgovernment"  # Key difference from commercial
  subscription_id = var.gov_subscription_id
  tenant_id       = var.gov_tenant_id
  client_id       = var.gov_client_id
  client_secret   = var.gov_client_secret
  features {}
}
EOF
ITAR (International Traffic in Arms Regulations) controls the export and import of defence-related articles, services, and technology. For software developers working on ITAR-regulated projects:
ITAR data cannot be processed in commercial Azure — even in US commercial regions. It must be processed in Azure Government, which restricts access to US persons.
Code, documentation, and technical data related to ITAR-controlled items are themselves ITAR-regulated. Storing them in a Git repository accessible to non-US persons is an export violation.
Azure Government's US-persons-only staff requirement satisfies the ITAR requirement that controlled data not be accessed by foreign nationals.

Cloud service providers that host ITAR data must be registered with the US State Department's Directorate of Defense Trade Controls (DDTC). Microsoft's ITAR registration covers Azure Government, not commercial Azure.

Development tools and CI/CD pipelines used for ITAR-regulated code must also run in the ITAR boundary — Azure Government has Azure DevOps government endpoints and GitHub Enterprise for US Government available.

Continuous integration and deployment pipelines for Azure Government workloads must also operate within the government boundary. The two common patterns are Azure DevOps Government (ado.gov) and GitHub Enterprise (self-hosted runners in an Azure Government network).

# Azure Pipelines: deploy to Azure Government
# (Pipeline runs in Azure DevOps Government — ado.gov, not dev.azure.com)

trigger:
  branches:
    include:
      - main

variables:
  - group: gov-deployment-secrets   # Key Vault-backed variable group in Azure Government
  - name: AZURE_ENVIRONMENT
    value: AzureUSGovernment        # Tells Azure CLI which cloud to target
  - name: RESOURCE_GROUP
    value: agency-prod-rg
  - name: LOCATION
    value: usgovvirginia

stages:
  - stage: Deploy
    displayName: Deploy to Azure Government
    jobs:
      - job: DeployInfrastructure
        pool:
          vmImage: ubuntu-latest
        steps:
          - task: AzureCLI@2
            displayName: 'Set Azure Government Cloud'
            inputs:
              azureSubscription: 'gov-azure-service-connection'
              scriptType: bash
              scriptLocation: inlineScript
              inlineScript: |
                az cloud set --name AzureUSGovernment
                az group create --name $(RESOURCE_GROUP) --location $(LOCATION)

          - task: AzureCLI@2
            displayName: 'Deploy Bicep template to USGov Virginia'
            inputs:
              azureSubscription: 'gov-azure-service-connection'
              scriptType: bash
              scriptLocation: inlineScript
              inlineScript: |
                az cloud set --name AzureUSGovernment
                az deployment group create \
                  --resource-group $(RESOURCE_GROUP) \
                  --template-file infra/main.bicep \
                  --parameters location=$(LOCATION) environment=production
Data Sovereignty vs. Data Residency: A Distinction Worth Making
The terms are related but distinct, and the difference matters when selecting between commercial and sovereign cloud:
a physical property — data is stored and processed within a specific geographic boundary. Commercial Azure's EU geography satisfies EU data residency requirements because GRS stays within the EEA.

a legal property — data is subject to the laws of a specific jurisdiction and is accessible only by entities with legal standing in that jurisdiction. Sovereign clouds go further than residency: they ensure that access to the infrastructure is controlled by screened nationals and that foreign governments cannot compel access through their own legal processes.

A US-based SaaS company storing EU customer PII in North Europe (EU geography commercial Azure) satisfies GDPR data residency. A US federal agency storing law enforcement data needs Azure Government — not just a US region — because residency alone does not satisfy the access control requirements of CJIS.

Data residency tells you WHERE the data is. Data sovereignty tells you WHO has jurisdiction over it and who can compel access to it. Sovereign clouds satisfy both; commercial Azure with geography selection satisfies residency but not the strictest sovereignty requirements.

Sovereign and government clouds are physically and logically separate instances of Azure, created for workloads that cannot share infrastructure with commercial cloud customers. Azure Government serves US federal, state, local, and tribal government agencies and their approved contractors, providing FedRAMP High, DoD IL4/IL5, CJIS, and ITAR certifications that are unavailable in commercial Azure. Azure China is operated by 21Vianet — not Microsoft — under Chinese telecommunications law, satisfying PIPL and MLPS 2.0 requirements for data that must remain in mainland China. Both sovereign clouds use different API endpoints, require separate accounts, and cannot be accessed from commercial Azure subscriptions. For most commercial organisations, commercial Azure with the appropriate geography selection (EU for GDPR, UK for UK GDPR, Australia East for Privacy Act) is sufficient — sovereign cloud is mandated by government compliance levels, not general security preference.
